data.title: Tracking Carl Icahn's 13F Report - Q1 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Carl Icahn’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Icahn’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 05/15/2025. Please visit our Tracking Carl Icahn’s Portfolio series to get an idea of his investment philosophy and our previous update for the fund’s moves during Q4 2024.

The number of holdings in the 13F filing remained steady at 14. The top five holdings in the filing are Icahn Enterprises, CVR Energy, Southwest Gas Holdings, CVR Partners, and International Flavors & Fragrances.

Carl Icahn is best known for building sizable stakes in businesses and then pushing for changes to increase shareholder value. To learn more about his investing style and philosophy check out “King Icahn: The Biography of a Renegade Capitalist”.

Note 1: On March 28, 2025, Icahn disclosed holding 27.46M shares (8.44% of business) of Endeavor Group. The acquisition of Endeavor Group by private equity firm Silverlake for $27.50 cash closed soon after. The idea is to join a lawsuit that seeks to revalue the shares at a higher price (appraisal rights).

Note 2: Icahn's 13F cannot be considered a listing of his holdings. Carl Icahn is the majority owner of Icahn Enterprises (NASDAQ:IEP) which holds several of the businesses and the entities in turn hold IEP. His 13F is a consolidated report. IEP Investor Presentation (Slide 5) has details of the business structure.

Stake Increases:

CVR Energy Inc. (CVI): CVI is a top three ~18% of the 13F portfolio position first purchased in Q4 2011. The bulk of the current position (66.69M shares: ~66% of the whole business) was purchased through a $30 per share tender offer in H1 2012. Q3 2023 saw a ~6% trimming at an average price of ~$35.20 per share. The stock currently trades at $23.22. There was a minor ~3% stake increase this quarter.

Note 1: Two MLPs were carved out since the 2012 tender: CVR Refining the refining portion and CVR Partners (UAN) the nitrogen fertilizer unit. CVR Energy had majority ownership stake in both. In August 2015, CVR Partners agreed to merge with Rentech Nitrogen. Q2 2018 saw a tender offer whereby new CVR Energy shares were exchanged for CVRR at a valuation of $24.26 per common unit (0.6335 shares of CVI for one CVRR). ~22M new shares were issued.

Note 2: Their ~$30 implied cost-basis is vastly overstated due to carve-outs and special dividends paid out over the years.

CVR Partners LP (UAN): CVR Energy has a ~38% ownership stake in CVR Partners and it is listed separately in the 13F report as of Q1 2024. UAN currently trades at $82.19. There was a minor ~5% stake increase during the last quarter and a marginal increase this quarter.

JetBlue Airways (JBLU): The 2.16% of the portfolio stake in JBLU was established during Q1 2024 at prices between $4.70 and $7.45. There was a ~90% stake increase this quarter at prices between $4.72 and $8.31. The stock currently trades near the low end of those ranges at $4.88.

Illumina Inc. (ILMN): ILMN stake was established during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$194 and ~$233. The position was sold down by ~90% during the last quarter at prices between $128.98 and $155.15. It was rebuilt this quarter at prices between ~$78 and ~$153. The stock currently trades near the low end of those ranges at $82.25.

Stake Decreases:

Southwest Gas Holdings (SWX): SWX is a 7.22% of the portfolio position established over the two quarters through Q3 2021 at prices between ~$63 and ~$72. There was a ~75% stake increase during Q3 2022 through a tender offer at $82.50 per share. That was followed by a ~60% further increase during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$59 and ~$67. The last quarter saw a ~13% reduction at prices between $67.33 and $78.53. That was followed by a ~22% selling this quarter at prices between ~$68 and ~$79. The stock currently trades at $71.48.

Note 1: They have a ~10% ownership stake in the business.

Note 2: The position was made public in an October 2022 regulatory filing when they said they were opposed to Southwest’s acquisition of Questar Pipeline, Dominion Energy’s (D) transportation and storage business.

Kept Steady:

Icahn Enterprises (IEP): IEP position has seen consistent increases over the years. The position size increased from ~98M shares to ~102M shares in 2013, to ~109M shares in 2014, to ~117M shares in 2015, to ~130M shares in 2016, to ~158M shares in 2017, 175.4M in 2018, 197M in 2019, 222M shares in 2020, 257M shares in 2021, 300M shares in 2022, and 450M shares as of Q1 2025. The stock currently trades at $8.45. This is compared to ~$6 NAV per share as of Q1 2025.

Note 1: The stake increase over the years is primarily due to them taking dividend consideration in additional shares rather than cash. The dividend was cut by 50% to $1 per unit in August 2023. It was cut by another 50% to 50c per unit in November 2024.

Note 2: During May 2023, IEP stock price dropped sharply following a short report from Hindenburg Research.

International Flavors & Fragrances (IFF): IFF is now a 3.89% of the portfolio stake. A small position was established in Q1 2022 at prices between ~$116 and ~$150. Q1 2024 saw the stake built at prices between ~$78 and ~$86. The stock currently trades near the low end of those ranges at $76.46.

Bausch Health (BHC): The ~3% of the portfolio BHC position was purchased in Q4 2020 at prices between ~$15.30 and ~$21 and increased by ~575% in the next quarter at prices between ~$21.25 and ~$34.40. The stock currently trades well below the low end of those ranges at $4.63.

Note: They own 34.7M shares (~10% of the business). Two Icahn nominees are on the board.

Dana Inc. (DAN): The 2.54% DAN stake was established in Q4 2020 at prices between ~$13.25 and ~$20 and increased by ~200% in the next quarter at prices between ~$19 and ~$27. Q3 2021 also saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between $20.90 and $24.60. The stock currently trades at $16.47.

Note: Icahn controls ~10% of Dana Inc.

American Electric Power (AEP): AEP is a small 1.76% stake established during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$71 and ~$84 and the stock currently trades well above that range at ~$102.

Caesars Entertainment (CZR): CZR is a 0.81% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2024 at prices between $31.85 and $43.98 and the stock currently trades well below that range at $25.63.

Note: It was reported in May that Ichan has established a stake in Caesar’s Entertainment (CZR) – he has no plans for activism this time around. From early 2019, he was involved in activism at Caesar’s. Details follow: They acquired ~66M shares of Caesars Entertainment (~10% of the business) in early 2019. This included ~5.8M shares in convertible bonds. Total outlay was $646M for a cost-basis of $9.78 per share. The position was since increased to ~120M shares of beneficial ownership (17.55% of business, majority through equity swaps and convertible bonds). Icahn pushed for a sale of the company and in April Anthony Rodio who previously ran Icahn’s Tropicana Entertainment was appointed CEO in a deal with the company. In June Eldorado Resorts (ERI) agreed to buy the company in a cash-and-stock deal ($8.40 cash and 0.0899 ERI shares for each share of old CZR held). The transaction closed in July 2020, and the combined business took the Caesar Entertainment name. Icahn exited the position soon after.

Sandridge Energy (SD): SD is a 0.73% of the portfolio activist stake established in Q4 2017 at prices between $16 and $21 and the stock is now at $10.38.

Note: Icahn has a ~13.5% ownership stake in the business. He lobbied the board and succeeded in ending the Bonanza Creek acquisition on December 28, 2017. In June 2018, Icahn gained control of Sandridge’s board by winning a proxy battle.

Bausch + Lomb Corp (BLCO): BLCO is a spinoff from Bausch Health that started trading in May 2022 at ~$18 per share. The stock currently trades at $11.61. In June 2022, two nominees of Icahn joined the board.

Centuri Holdings (CTRI): The small 0.54% CTRI stake was established during Q2 2024 at $21 per share. CTRI is a carve-out from Southwest Gas that started trading in April. Icahn acquired the shares through a concurrent private placement that closed immediately following the IPO. Shares started trading at ~$24 and is currently at $22.64. Q3 2024 saw a ~40% stake increase at prices between $14.92 and $21.45 while during the last quarter there was a ~30% reduction at prices between $15.51 and $21.75.

Note: Icahn also owns ~90% of Viskase Companies (OTCPK:VKSC). He is also known to have a position in Fannie/Freddie (OTCQB:FNMA) (OTCQB:FMCC).

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Icahn’s 13F stock holdings in Q1 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Carl Icahn’s 13F filings for Q4 2024 and Q1 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EchoStar Corporation Has Entered The End Game (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
To be frank with you, things have not been going very well for shareholders of EchoStar Corporation (NASDAQ:SATS) recently. In fact, they haven't been going well for quite some time. Since I last reaffirmed the company as a Sell candidate back in October of 2024, shares have fallen by 20.7%. That plunge is a world apart from the 4.2% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) enjoyed over the same window of time. This decline occurred even after factoring in the 17.5% rise that shares experienced on June 5th. That followed an 11.3% drop that the stock experienced on June 3rd after management announced that it was withholding payment on interest for some of its debt because of uncertainty caused by a regulatory issue that could affect its spectrum.

This alone poses a threat for shareholders. But in all honesty, even if this was not an immediate problem, it looks as though the company's days are numbered. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all declining materially. Yes, shares of the business look attractive in some respects. But when you consider that almost all parts of the company are continuing to worsen across the board, especially the largest part that is its Pay-TV segment, I honestly see no redeeming qualities here. In light of the challenges that the company has faced, I believe that downgrading it from a Sell to a Strong Sell is the appropriate choice right now.

A multitude of problems

Operationally speaking, EchoStar is not doing well right now. In the chart above, as an example, you can see financial results for the last three completed fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see results for the first quarter of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024.

What we see here is a consistent decline almost across the board. Admittedly, net profits have been a bit inconsistent. But when it comes to revenue and every measure of cash flow that the company has, the situation has only worsened.

This is not the picture of a company that is doing well. I have, in the past, bought into turn around prospects and achieved significant success in the process. But in those cases, there are certain attributes that, here, are missing. For instance, in those other opportunities, there is often some big part of the company that is actually doing well. That is not the case here. Do you see what I mean?

We should probably dig into each of the three operating segments that the business has.

The first and largest of these is the Pay-TV segment. This is the part of the company that provides pay TV services under the DISH and SLING brands. It also focuses to some extent on the designing, developing, and distributing of receiver systems, plus it provides digital broadcast operations like satellite uplinking and downlinking, transmission, and other services, to third-party pay TV providers. This is largely focused around the DISH brand, while the SLING brand provides multi-channel, live-linear, and on-demand streaming over-the-top internet based video programming services.

From 2022 through 2024, revenue for this segment contracted from $12.51 billion to $10.69 billion. This decline in sales brought with it lower profits and cash flows as well. As an example, operating income contracted from $2.93 billion to $2.65 billion, while OIBDA fell from $3.36 billion to $2.99 billion. This weakening for the segment can be attributed to the fact that subscriber count for the company has fallen from 9.75 million to 7.78 million. This was more than enough to offset the rise in monthly ARPU from $101.20 to $108.90.

For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the picture worsened also. Revenue of $2.54 billion fell short of the $2.73 billion generated the previous year. Thanks to this, operating income contracted from $670.1 million to $653.4 million while OIBDA dropped from $755.5 million to $729.9 million. A decline in the number of subscribers from 8.18 million to 7.40 million caused this drama and had a significantly more negative impact than the positive impact from a rise in monthly ARPU from $107.38 to $110.64.

This has not been the only part of the company that has suffered. From 2022 through 2024, the firm’s Wireless segment reported a decline in revenue from $4.18 billion to $3.61 billion. This drop has brought with it a worsening in the firm's operating income from negative $888.2 million to negative $2.83 billion. Meanwhile, its OIBDA worsened from negative $599.6 million to negative $1.70 billion. During this time, the company saw a drop in the number of wireless subscribers from 7.98 million to 7 million. It also saw a drop in monthly ARPU from $37.72 to $36.57.

For those not familiar with the company, it is worth mentioning that the Wireless segment for the business focuses on providing wireless communication services and products to its customers, with the big push for the company being its 5G voice over new radio (or VoNR) offering and its 5G broadband service. These services are provided under its Boost Mobile and Gen Mobile brands.

For the first quarter of 2025, revenue was $972.8 million. That was actually up from the $914 million reported the previous year and was driven by a rise in monthly ARPU from $36.69 to $37.89. However, the number of subscribers dropped year over year from 7.30 million to 7.15 million. There is some good news here. The number of subscribers that the company had under this segment was up about 150,000 on a net basis compared to the previous quarter. Management attributed that to lower rates of churn and a rise in government subsidized subscribers. Most of the revenue increase, however, came from additional equipment sales and other revenue. This was because of a rise in the number of units shipped and wireless devices, not to mention a growth in revenue per unit shipped caused by changes in unit mix.

This can all be attributed to the quarter over quarter rise in subscriber count. But this improvement in revenue did not stop the company from reporting a worsening in profitability, with its operating loss growing from $645.2 million to $722.3 million, and it's OIBDA worsening from negative $363.5 million to negative $415.1 million.

There is also the Broadband and Satellite Services segment. Through this unit, the company provides broadband services to its customers, as well as certain satellite related services that helped to facilitate communications. From 2022 through 2024, revenue for this segment contracted from $2 billion to $1.58 billion. That caused a worsening in its operating income from $181.6 million to negative $117.9 million. At the same time, it saw a reduction in OIBDA from $644.4 million to $341.9 million. All of this pain was attributable to a drop in the number of subscribers from 1.23 million to 883,000.

Continued pain came in the first quarter of 2025, with revenue of $370.7 million falling short of the $382.6 million reported one year ago. We did see profitability metrics improve, with the firm's operating loss narrowing from $39.6 million to $19.2 million, and with its OIBDA improving from $79.3 million to $85.7 million. But with the number of subscribers contracting from 978,000 last year to 853,000 this year, the overall trend here is clear.

In the past, there was some evidence that a turnaround for the company might be possible. Last October, for instance, I wrote about the firm's decision to sell its video distribution business, which included DISH TV and Sling TV in exchange for $1. While this sounds absurd, the transaction also brought with it a meaningful reduction in debt. The buyer, DIRECTV had agreed to assume all of the debt of the business. And when you factor in other changes that would have been brought to the table, the end result would have been a $7 billion reduction in net debt. It would have also relieved the company of another $11.6 billion in future obligations that would have proven helpful for the company. But in late November of last year, the buyer terminated the purchase agreement because certain exchange offers that the company deemed necessary in order for the deal to go through ended up not being consummated.

Investors who are bullish about the company might also point out that there is significant potential in the 5G side of things. The firm has, in recent years, invested around $30 billion into wireless spectrum. And it has been focusing on growing its 5G subscriber base. The company had a strict timeline in which to achieve certain coverage obligations covering 80% of the U.S. population in each "Economic Area" defined by the FCC. That was supposed to be achieved at the end of December of last year.

And on January 10th, in a filing to the FCC, EchoStar certified that it had achieved 5G broadband service coverage for at least 85% of the population in each "Economic Area." According to management, it boasts 5G VoNR coverage of over 220 million Americans and 5G broadband service for more than 268 million. But even with that kind of coverage, the company is still losing ground fast, with revenue, profits, and cash flows, all contracting sharply. There is an opportunity for the company to transition more of its users from utilizing network services under agreements that it has with T-Mobile USA (TMUS) and AT&T (T), which could help it. But I don't see it making a great deal of difference.

But even to the extent that this might prove positive for shareholders, the firm is now facing additional challenges. Recently, news broke that the FCC is investigating whether EchoStar actually did hit the requirements to deploy 5G that it had been forced to meet. This could lead to significant risks for the company and has caused the firm, citing uncertainty for its business, to miss not one, but two, interest payments on some of its debts.

In a filing that came out on June 2nd, management said that they were holding off on paying $183 million in cash interest payments on some of their debts. This followed management's decision to delay payment on other debt, with that delayed payment amounting to interest of $326 million. These technically amount to defaults on their debt and start a 30-day grace period whereby the firm can either make the payments as it is contractually obligated to, or be pushed into bankruptcy.

While the firm could potentially receive a waiver on these payments, none of this changes the fact that the overall picture for the company is getting worse. In the chart above, you can see its net debt and its net leverage ratio from 2022 through 2024. You can also see estimates for 2025 if net debt remains unchanged from where it was in the first quarter of this year and if we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year when it comes to EBITDA.

In the chart below, you can see how the company is priced using these estimates and using historical results from 2024. The elevated EV to EBITDA multiple, which is far higher than the price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple, illustrates why a multifactorial approach to valuing companies is important and it also demonstrates the precarious situation that the business is in. Even if shares were materially cheaper, I wouldn't say that anything less than a bearish assessment would be appropriate given the totality of the circumstances.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I think that things are going to get worse for EchoStar Corporation from this point on. I honestly don't see any redeeming qualities in the business at this time. Fundamentally, the firm is a mess, and financial performance is probably going to continue to worsen. On top of this, there is now the prospect of bankruptcy if it does not start paying its interest as it is contractually obligated to do. At the end of the day, I think that these challenges necessitate a downgrade to a Strong Sell.

data.title: Broadcom: Strong Growth From This AI Winner
data.description:
Article Thesis

Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) reported its most recent earnings results on Thursday afternoon. While the company beat estimates on both lines while also providing appealing guidance numbers for the current quarter, AVGO shares do seem somewhat pricey, which is why I do not see Broadcom as a great investment at current prices.

Past Coverage

I have written about Broadcom here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently late last year, when I covered Broadcom's deal with Apple (AAPL). In that article, I was bearish on AAPL, which is down since then, while I was more optimistic, although not overly bullish, on Broadcom. Around half a year has passed since then, which was eventful for the company -- it reported earnings results twice, including today, and has seen its shares trade down to below $150 before shares soared to the $260s again.

With two quarters having passed since my last article on AVGO, and with the company reporting its most recent earnings results on Thursday, it is time for an update.

What Happened?

On Thursday afternoon, following the market's close, Broadcom reported its fiscal Q2 results. These were the headline numbers:

We see that Broadcom generated highly appealing revenue growth, which was not a surprise, of course, thanks to the company's exposure to macro tailwinds such as cloud computing and AI. Growth was marginally better than expected, at just above 20%. The company also beat the consensus earnings per share estimate slightly, but the market didn't like these results a lot -- at the time of writing, AVGO trades down by a couple of percentage points. Let's delve into the results.

Broadcom's Strong Q2 And Positive Outlook

Tech is a high-growth industry, but still, most tech companies do not grow by 20% per year. Of course, outliers such as Nvidia (NVDA) exist, but apart from the king of AI, major tech companies mostly generate lower growth compared to Broadcom. The Magnificent 7, for example, grew like this over the last year:

Nvidia aside, the Mag 7 companies mostly grew their revenues by 5% to 15% in the most recent quarter, with Tesla (TSLA) being an outlier to the downside due to its substantial revenue decline. Relative to these companies, Broadcom's growth looks quite compelling -- maybe one of the reasons why some believe that Broadcom, not Tesla, should be a member of the Mag 7 companies.

Broadcom's revenue growth was broad-based, with both business units delivering strong results, as we can see in the following table:

Semiconductor solutions, Broadcom's bigger unit, grew at a slightly slower but still appealing rate of 17% compared to one year earlier, while the somewhat smaller Infrastructure software business grew by a very nice 25% year-over-year. This unit, which saw a major boost from the VMware acquisition that closed in 2023, is gaining relevance for Broadcom. As its relative contribution to AVGO's overall revenues grows, Broadcom's overall revenue growth rate could be positively impacted. Broadcom's guidance for the current quarter, its fiscal Q3, implies revenue growth of ~20% as well, in line with the second quarter.

Management noted that Broadcom's AI revenue, which is not broken out into a distinct unit, grew by a very nice 44% compared to one year earlier, with AI revenue coming in at $4.4 billion for the quarter, or close to a third of AVGO's total revenues. This isn't surprising, of course, as the heavy investments in AI data centers by companies such as Meta Platforms (META) or Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) do not only drive demand for Nvidia's chips, but also for other AI hardware such as Broadcom's switches. With the recent news about the release of its new high-powered Tomahawk 6 switch just two days ago, Broadcom should be well-positioned to capture a significant portion of the growing data center hardware market in the future, too. I thus believe that AI will continue to remain a growth driver for Broadcom in the future.

Sales are important, but so are margins and profits. Here, things looked really good, at least when we focus on Broadcom's adjusted results. The company hit an adjusted EBITDA margin of 67%, which is pretty appealing, and since depreciation and amortization are relatively low while AVGO's tax rate is low as well, Broadcom's adjusted net margin came in at a very strong 52%. That compares very favorably to the net margins by other major tech companies, including the Magnificent 7 -- not even Nvidia's net margin was this high, as it came in at 45% during the most recent quarter.

Thanks to a combination of compelling revenue growth and a strong margin performance, Broadcom was able to grow its adjusted earnings per share by 44% during the second quarter, which was more than twice the company's revenue growth rate over the same period. Clearly, Broadcom has substantial pricing power, which is why its EBITDA margins are so high, while operating leverage allows the company to grow its operating and net margins as its business scales up.

When we look at GAAP results, things do not look quite as good, however. GAAP earnings are way lower than adjusted earnings -- GAAP earnings per share were $1.03 for Q2, which is not even two-thirds of the adjusted result of $1.59. It makes sense to adjust for some items that are not recurring, such as legal expenses or takeover closing costs. But other expenses are recurring and excluding them from adjusted results paints a potentially too-bright picture.

One of these recurring expenses that Broadcom excludes from its adjusted results is share-based compensation expenses. Broadcom is by far not the only company that excludes those, as many other companies do so as well -- but in AVGO's case, those are pretty meaningful, and due to the dilution that share issuance to management and employees causes, share-based compensation is an expense with a substantial "real-world" impact. Share-based compensation expenses totaled $1.8 billion in Q2, or close to 40% of the company's GAAP net profit of $5.0 billion -- while some may disagree, I believe that this is an expense that shouldn't be ignored.

The impact of Broadcom's hefty pace of share issuance as a form of compensation can be seen when we look at Broadcom's share count. During the second quarter, the share count averaged 4.826 billion shares, up half a percentage point over the last year -- despite Broadcom spending $4.2 billion on share repurchases during the second quarter alone, and a total of $9 billion over the last year. When a company spends close to a billion dollars on buybacks every month and its share count is still climbing, that shouldn't be ignored, I think.

Broadcom: Growth Appeal, But Pricy Valuation

Broadcom has seen its shares roughly double from the April lows, which naturally has made its shares a lot pricier than they were two months ago. Based on current earnings per share estimates for this year, Broadcom trades for 39x net profits. Analysts are using adjusted earnings per share estimates, which means that the GAAP earnings multiple would be considerably higher -- if the ratio of GAAP earnings to adjusted earnings remains stable throughout the remainder of the year, the GAAP earnings multiple would be in the 60x range.

The company's enterprise value to EBITDA ratio, which accounts for changes in debt usage and for cash held on the balance sheet, is quite high as well:

At 31x, the EV/EBITDA ratio is high in absolute terms, and it is high relative to where it was in the recent past -- which is not too surprising, as AVGO rallied by almost 100% over the last year alone.

I believe that Broadcom Inc. will continue to deliver appealing business growth in the foreseeable future, thanks to AI tailwinds and its strong Infrastructure software business. But I'm not willing to pay almost 40x adjusted net profits for that, with massive share-based compensation expenses not even included in that number yet. AVGO surely was a compelling buy in April -- unfortunately, I missed it there, as I bought some other stocks -- but right here, I don't consider it a buy.

data.title: I Prefer Suncor Energy Over Williams Companies Amid OPEC+ Production Increase
data.description:
When I last wrote on Suncor Energy (NYSE:SU) in mid-April, I compared it to Petrobras (PBR) and rated it as a buy. The key considerations are Ray Dalio’s investing principles such as geographical diversification and commodity exposure with the trade disputes that started in early April. As for the Williams Companies (NYSE:WMB), I last wrote on it in mid-March with a hold rating after considering the prospects of domestic natural gas production.

Since then, a few new catalysts have emerged for both companies and also for the energy sector as a whole. The remainder of this article will focus on the top change in each bucket. For their business-specific changes, I will provide my thoughts after reviewing their FQ1 earnings reports. For sector-wide catalysts, I will focus on the potential impacts from the recent announcement of OPEC+ to increase oil production. After examining these changes, my conclusion is that the market has exaggerated the impact of oil price on SU stock. As such, I see a valuation gap between SU and WMB that's too wide to justify, resulting in a return/risk profile far more skew for SU than WMB. For these considerations, I keep rating on SU as a buy and WMB as a hold.

Let me start with the Q1 reports for SU and WMB to prime the discussion of oil price impact. As you can see from the following snapshots, both companies released earnings in early May, and both of them posted better-than-expected EPS. For SU, its normalized EPS came in at $0.95, beating Wall Street’s estimate by $00.4 per share. WMB’s results beat the Street’s by a moderate margin. However, the stock prices have diverged considerably, as seen in the second chart below. To wit, WMB has delivered a remarkable price return of 45.29% in the past year, showing a consistent general upward momentum. In contrast, SU has experienced considerable downward pressure and delivered a negative price return of -3.08% in this period.

Next, I will argue that this diverge is too large to justify.

SU and WMB Stock: Oil Price Impacts

A large factor behind the above price divergence is the different sensitivity of their revenues with respect to oil prices, which have been under pressure lately. To further compound the price pressure on oil, last Saturday, key OPEC+ member countries decided to increase their oil production starting in July 2025. More specifics of their scheduled increases are shown in the chart below. In the end, these member countries plan to boost their oil production by ~411k barrels per day starting in July. Crude oil prices were already under pressure before this increase, and I expect this OPEC+ decision to further heighten the pressure.

With these developments, I can see why some degree of price or valuation divergence between SU and WMB is justifiable. Williams is primarily an energy infrastructure company. Its revenue model is largely fee-based, derived from gathering, processing, transporting, and storage. Thus, there are good reasons for its stock price showing less sensitivity to these above developments. In contrast, a substantial part of Suncor's revenue is directly tied to crude oil and refined product prices, as well as production volumes. However, I think the divergence shown above reflects an overreaction for at least two reasons.

First, I think the market might have overestimated the correlation between SU’s earnings and oil prices. SU is an integrated oil company with both upstream and downstream segments. Oil prices changes tend to impact the upstream segment and downstream segment in opposite directions. For example, if the OPEC+’s decision indeed keeps oil price pressured, I do expect its upstream segment to suffer reduced profits. However, I would anticipate the downstream operations to benefit in this case, as lower crude oil prices tend to lower the cost of their feeds. Lower prices of their refinery feeds can lead to better profit margins for these segments and counterbalance the profit pressure from the upstream side.

As a reflection of the above counterbalance mechanisms, the next figure displays the correlation between SU’s cash from operations in the past with respect to Brent Crude prices. The data spans about three decades. It's true that SU’s operating cash has exhibited significant positive correlation over extended periods in the past. But the average correlation over the long run is quite weak at 0.045 as seen.

SU vs. WMB: Valuation Discount Is Too Wide To Justify

Second, I also consider the valuation gap between SU and WMB too wide to justify. SU is undervalued in comparison to WMB by a wide margin, as you can see from the following table. To wit, SU P/E ratios are less than half of WMB’s. As an example, its TTM P/E ratio is in the single digits only (9.69x), while WMB is trading at over 31x. Its FY1 P/E ratio of 12.1x is almost less than half of WMB’s 28.5x.

Yet, SU offers very comparable profitability metrics when compared to WMB, as you can see from the next two screenshots. For example, SU’s gross profit margin is 58.6%, essentially on par with WMB’s 59.9%. Going down the earnings statement, the comparison varies. But again, I see a very similar profit profile overall, certainly not drastically different enough to cause a 2x to 3x difference in their P/E ratios. For example, WMB indeed boasts a far stronger net income margin of 20.6%, compared to SU’s 12%. However, SU makes it up with the FCF margin of almost 16%, slightly better than WMB’s 7.25%.

Broadening the timescale a bit, SU has also demonstrated a better return on invested capital (ROIC) in the longer term, as seen in the second chart. Its current ROIC hovers around 11.03%, significantly above its average of 5.95%. In the past 10 years, SU's ROIC has averaged 5.95%, also surpassing WMB’s average of 2.87%.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Of course, WMB is a solid investment idea in its own right and offers many positives too. As aforementioned, its fee-based model makes is far less exposed to commodity pricing than SU (or other similar stocks). Such reduced exposure can translate into far lower volatility risks. As a reflection, the table below chart compares the beta of SU and WMB in the past two and five years. As you can easily see, WMB has exhibited far lower beta in response to market movements when compared to SU. For instance, its 24-month beta is 0.64 and 60-month beta is 0.68, only about half of SU’s 60-month beta of 1.21.

As another catalyst for WMB, the company is changing up its leadership. More specifically, Executive Vice President Chad Zamarin will take the role of CEO starting in July 2025. Zamarin has been at Williams since 2017, leading innovative energy solutions and helping data centers meet their power demands. As such, I expect him to emphasize initiatives in these future growth areas in the years to come.

To conclude, this article is promoted by two new catalysts since my last writings: The FQ1 earnings reports provided by SU and WMB, and also OPEC+’s decision to increase oil production. I do see reasons why these developments could pressure SU more than WMB. However, I think the market has overacted, resulting in a valuation gap between SU and WMB that's too large to justify, in my view. This has led me to see a far more attractive return/risk profile in SU than in WMB.

data.title: The Preferred Stock Of Wells Fargo Is Offering A 52-Week High Dividend Yield Of 6.6%
data.description:
Almost two years ago, I recommended buying the preferred stock of Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC.PR.L) for its attractive 6.5% dividend and my expectations for a moderation of interest rates. Since that article, the stock has offered a total return of 13%, as it has remained essentially flat and has rewarded the shareholders only through its dividend payments. Nevertheless, this is a decent return for a fixed-income security.

The investment thesis played out really well until the elections that took place late last year. Inflation moderated last year and thus the Fed began cutting interest rates in September 2024. As a result, the preferred stock of Wells Fargo rallied 12% between my aforementioned article and September 2024, thus offering an exceptional total return (22%) until that time.

However, the preferred stock has pared all its capital gains since then due to the surge of treasury yields, which has resulted primarily from the tariffs announced by the new U.S. government. If the government delivers on its promise to impose high tariffs on imported goods, it will probably cause inflation to resurge. As a result, the Fed will not be able to resume cutting interest rates. Instead, the central bank may even have to raise interest rates in order to tame inflation in such an adverse scenario.

The current yield of the preferred stock of Wells Fargo of 6.6% is attractive from a long-term perspective, as the dividend is essentially safe, but many investors hesitate to lock in the 52-week high yield of the stock due to the somewhat unpredictable effect of the policy of the U.S. government on inflation and interest rates.

However, the government is likely to reduce government spending and raise some taxes at some point in order to reduce the budget deficit and prevent debt from rising too fast. The U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio has climbed to a nearly 60-year high of 124% while the budget deficit has remained -6% in each of the last two years. These two figures constitute the other reason (apart from the threat of tariffs) behind the nearly 17-year high level of the U.S. 10-year bond yield (4.43%).

In order to reduce this yield and prevent its interest expense from surging, the U.S. government will almost certainly have to reduce its spending and raise some taxes at some point. Consequently, the economy is likely to decelerate and interest rates are likely to moderate and thus provide a tailwind to fixed-income securities, such as the preferred stock of Wells Fargo. Therefore, investors should lock in the 6.6% dividend of the preferred stock and wait patiently for potential capital gains to materialize.

Risk #1: The issuer

Before buying a preferred stock, investors should first evaluate the risk of a potential cut or suspension of the preferred dividend. However, in the case of Wells Fargo, the preferred dividend is as safe as investors can hope for.

Wells Fargo has remained profitable every single year over the last decade. Apart from 2020, when it reported earnings per share of only $0.43 due to the pandemic, it has posted earnings per share of at least $3.30 every year since 2015.

Moreover, while the bank is not in high-growth mode right now, it exhibits positive business momentum, at least with respect to the safety of its preferred dividend. In the first quarter of 2025, its net interest income dipped 6% over the prior year’s quarter but its non-interest income edged up 0.2% while its non-interest expense decreased 3% and its provisions for loan losses declined 1%.

Source: Investor Presentation

Given also lower income tax expense, normalized earnings per share grew 1.6% over the prior year's quarter, from $1.26 to $1.28, and exceeded the analysts' estimates by $0.06. The bank has beaten the analysts' earnings-per-share estimates for 19 consecutive quarters. Analysts expect Wells Fargo to grow its earnings per share 1% this year, from $5.60 to $5.65. Even better, they expect the bank to grow its bottom line at a double-digit rate in 2026 and 2027.

Moreover, the payout ratio of the common dividend is just 30%. Thanks to the healthy payout ratio and the positive growth prospects of Wells Fargo, its common dividend appears safe. Even in a worst-case scenario of a fierce recession, the bank is highly unlikely to eliminate its common dividend. As long as the company does not eliminate its common dividend, it does not have the right to reduce its preferred dividend.

It is also worth noting that Wells Fargo has paid $5.2 billion in common dividends and $1.1 billion in preferred dividends in the last 12 months. In other words, preferred dividends currently comprise just 17.5% of the total dividend payments of the company. Therefore, if Wells Fargo needs to preserve some funds in the event of a severe downturn, it will have ample room to preserve funds by cutting its common dividend. To cut a long story short, the 6.6% preferred dividend of Wells Fargo is as safe as an investor can hope for in my view.

Risk #2: Interest rates

The primary risk factor for the preferred stock of Wells Fargo is the future path of interest rates. Higher interest rates exert pressure on fixed-income securities, such as preferred stocks, as investors can identify high yields elsewhere.

The U.S. government has announced that it may implement excessive tariffs to the imported goods of many countries in an effort to reduce imports and support the domestic industry. If it imposes high tariffs, it will probably provide a boost to inflation and thus the Fed, which aims to restore inflation to 2.0%-2.5%, is not likely to cut interest rates. This adverse scenario is the reason behind the 11% correction of the preferred stock of Wells Fargo off its peak before the elections.

However, this scenario has become much less likely after the fierce sell-off of U.S. treasuries that took place immediately after the Liberation Day. If the yield of U.S. treasuries surges, it will greatly increase the annual interest expense of the U.S. government, which is already standing at an all-time high.

Source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis

As a result, the efforts of the U.S. government on reducing the budget deficit by reducing imports of goods will be wasted due to increased interest expense. This explains the softening of the stance of the government since early April. In essence, the government is a “hostage” of the bond market and hence it cannot be as aggressive as it was in its first three months at the office.

Notably, the stock market has retrieved nearly all the losses it incurred after the Liberation Day but bonds have remained suppressed. To be sure, the U.S. 10-year bond yield is currently standing at a nearly 17-year high of 4.43%. This does not bode well for the interest expense of the U.S. If this yield remains elevated, the government will have to borrow funds at high interest rates and thus its interest expense will significantly increase.

Moreover, as mentioned in the introduction, the U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio has climbed to a nearly 60-year high of 124% while the budget deficit has remained -6% in each of the last two years. These two figures are key factors behind the surge of the U.S. 10-year bond yield. In order to drive this yield down and keep its interest expense under control, the U.S. government will almost certainly have to reduce its spending and raise some taxes at some point. When that happens, the U.S. economy will probably decelerate and thus the Fed is likely to resume cutting interest rates. Such a development will provide a strong tailwind to the preferred stock of Wells Fargo. Overall, the preferred stock of Wells Fargo is offering a safe 6.6% dividend and has good chances of offering capital gains as well whenever the U.S. government adopts drastic measures to reduce the budget deficit.

Risk

A typical risk factor for most stocks is a recession but this is not the case for the preferred stock of Wells Fargo. In the event of a recession, the Fed will almost certainly cut interest rates and thus it will provide a strong tailwind to the preferred stock of Wells Fargo.

The primary risk factor for the preferred stock of Wells Fargo is the adverse scenario of a resurgence of inflation. If the government imposes hefty tariffs on the trading partners of the country, inflation is likely to rise significantly. As a result, the Fed will not cut interest rates. Instead, it may even have to raise interest rates if inflation surges out of control. Fortunately, this scenario has low odds of materializing, as the yields of U.S. bonds will probably surge in such a scenario and the government will almost certainly do its best to avoid such a disastrous development.

Final thoughts

The preferred stock of Wells Fargo is offering a 52-week high dividend yield of 6.6%. The yield is so high primarily due to the surge of the yield of U.S. 10-year bonds. However, such a high yield of U.S. bonds is not sustainable over the long run, as it will enhance the budget deficit of the government via high interest expense. Therefore, the U.S. government is likely to do its best to reduce its budget deficit at some point in the future.

When that happens, bond yields are likely to moderate off nearly 17-year highs and thus they will provide a tailwind to the preferred stock of Wells Fargo. If the preferred stock reverts to its level in September 2024, it will offer capital gains of 12%. Overall, income-oriented investors should lock in the 6.6% dividend yield of the preferred stock of Wells Fargo before it reverts to lower levels.

data.title: Marvell Technology: Don't Get Tempted By The 42% Dip
data.description:
Marvell Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL) is down 42% YTD, and the company’s 1QFY26 report, which came out at the end of May, didn’t help reverse this downward spiral; in fact, losses deepened post-earnings. We don’t see any near-term catalyst to turn around Marvell’s fortunes in the near-term, so we reiterate Sell on the stock.

What investors need to understand about Marvell is that its stock rallied late last year, going from $56 per share in August to about $124 in December. The stock was on a streak due to its custom AI ASIC, but for the last two earnings calls it’s disappointed, which is easy to mistake for a case of the weakness being priced in, but we think Marvell will continue to struggle in the near term after last year’s rally put a lot of eyes on the stock as an underrated AI play. While that was true then, it is no longer applicable today, and there are three reasons why.

Reason #1: Expectations are (still) much too high

Marvell jumped onto the AI bandwagon on demand for its electro-optical position and ramp of its custom ASIC; these two get factored into the company’s data center-related sales, which have been growing sequentially from $816.4M in 1QFY25 to $1.44B in 1QFY26.

The good news is that Marvell is benefiting from increased port count and expansion of the electro-optical market; the bad news is that this positive already got priced into the stock and Street expectations for Marvell’s AI revenue growth, which now sit at 40% for the current year, higher than what we think is realistic. We’ve been flagging this high expectation for a while, but after we got insight into the first two quarters of Marvell’s FY26, we are confident that this number is much too high. For the quarter reported in May, Marvell told us that its data center-related revenues grew 5.5% sequentially, marking the slowest sequential growth since 1QFY24, which is now two years ago, and management is expecting data center-related revenues to only grow another 5% next quarter. This would mean that 2HFY26 would have to have hyper growth for the company to meet, let alone outperform, Wall Street’s 40% expectation.

The chart below outlines Marvell’s quarterly revenue by business line, and this chart is meant to show you two things. The first is that the pace of growth in the company’s data center-related sales has moderated for the past two quarters, which has been consistent with the stock selling off after each of those quarters. We expect this slower pace to continue as Marvell’s largest ASIC customer, Amazon (AMZN), has already been priced into outlook.

The company has two other ASIC customers, but guess what? No one does single source anymore, meaning none of the hyperscalers put all their eggs in one basket, so Marvell’s ASIC customers, for the most part, are also Broadcom (AVGO) customers, and Broadcom, unlike Marvell, has scale on its side.

The second thing this chart should show is the pace of growth in enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure sales; these two markets entered correction during 1HFY25 and have only recently begun to recover. Now, in spite of their recovery over the past two quarters, the stock has sold off, which means that the market is more interested in Marvell’s data center sales than anything else. This means that if the data center pace of growth doesn’t pick up, then Marvell will be looking at an even sharper disappointment come 2HFY26.

Reason #2: Gross margins are getting slim for the summer

Now, let’s say Marvell grows its ASIC business, which sounds like it should be the best case scenario for the company, what would happen? The answer is pretty simple: margins would take a hit. The problem for Marvell is that they do not have the benefit of scale or economies of scale, for that matter, meaning when they scale their ASIC business, their margins contract. Marvell’s margins have already been contracting for the past five quarters consecutively, as shown below.

The reason for the contraction is the ASIC business. Management reported non-GAAP gross margin of 59.8%, contracting 30 basis points due to ASIC representing a higher percent of the sales mix. Heading into Q2, management is guiding for gross margins to remain in the 59% to 60% range. The problem is that gross margin contraction is an inescapable part of ASIC business; management got asked about this on their earnings call in May by Timothy Arcuri from UBS; Tim asked about AI as part of the data center mix and if lower gross margins were the result of custom silicon growing faster during the quarter. Management, Matt Murphy, to be specific, handled the question, answering that:

...clearly the custom business we have does run at a fundamentally lower gross margin. So that is going to modulate our gross margins on a quarterly basis and you've seen the impact, but we've also had, I think, strong recovery in some of the other businesses as well. But that's always been fundamentally the custom business a lower than company average gross margin.

Unlike Broadcom, Marvell’s other end markets cannot offset this. Enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure are recovering, but their strength is not sufficient to support margin expansion. This is especially when we remember management told us that “Data center was our largest end market contributing 76% of total revenue” in this earnings call. Of that data center revenue, data center AI made up most of the mix, speculatively at +55% of the mix this quarter.

So, all in all, if Marvell is to meet Street expectations for AI revenue growth, it needs to expand ASIC business, and if it does that, margins will take a hit. Don't take it from us, take it from management. In another section of the Q&A, Murphy added that:

...within custom, there is a range, and it's like the old adage in semiconductors. The higher the volume, you're going to see lower gross margins.

Reason #3: Pricey, pricey, pricey

Marvell is expensive; the stock trades above the peer group average on almost every metric. On forward EV/Sales, Marvell trades at 6.97x compared to a group average of 2.82x, and on forward P/E, 60.18x versus a group average of 28.38x. The chart below shows the full scoop on Marvell’s multiples.

The stock is up over the past month by 7%, but this is part of the broader market recovery after the Trump tariff panic rather than a specific sign of momentum for Marvell, in our opinion. If you look at the company’s closest competitor, Broadcom, the stock is one of the best-performing semi stocks, up 57% since the start of the quarter, versus Marvell, up 8% during the same period. To top it off, Marvell’s RSI is also closer to overbought territory than oversold, currently at 58, which makes the case to buy on weakness after the YTD pullback all the more unattractive in our opinion.

Bottom Line: Great company, wrong time

Marvell is a great company; we're fans. That said, we don't see an attractive risk-reward for the stock this year. The risk for more pressure on its financials outweighs its position within the AI market at the moment. We think it'll be increasingly hard for them to impress investors unless they get an additional ASIC customer, and even then, that'll come at the expense of their margins. Every way we look at it, Marvell could see more downside ahead, even though a lot of the weakness has been priced in after the YTD drop.

data.title: Marimekko Starts FY25 On Good Footing But Is More Expensive Than LVMH
data.description:
Marimekko’s (OTCPK:MKKOF) 1Q25 results show a relatively resilient operation in most geographies, with a positive outlook despite seasonal or non-recurrent factors affecting margins, particularly in the high-margin licensing business.

Sales continued to grow in key geographies and management reaffirmed guidance for the year, both in revenue and margins. This is a positive signal in a weak global apparel environment.

Unfortunately, the stock has barely moved since my last article and continues to trade at a level I consider unreasonably high for an apparel brand. I maintain my Hold.

1Q25 results

Global growth: Marimekko posted sales growth across all its key geographies.

In Finland, its largest market, the company reported an increase despite tough comps due to a more promotional wholesale environment last year. Retail sales in Finland were up 9%.

In Asia-Pacific, Marimekko’s most important market at the brand level (though not in consolidated financials due to partner-operated stores), growth continued ex-licensing. Retail sales were up 16% (online stores operated by Marimekko) and wholesale was up 4% (partner stores). Brand sales, which include partner retail sales and licensing product sales, were down 6%. The company's APAC revenue declined 2%, however, due to a large drop in licensing income.

Other markets, including Scandinavia, continental Europe, and North America, developed positively as well. While they represent smaller absolute contributions (25% of revenues in aggregate), they still show that the company is performing in several markets.

Licensing hit: The main drag on 1Q25 performance was the sharp drop in licensing income (-70% aggregate and -100% in APAC, as shown on the earnings report).

This had been flagged in the 4Q24 report and again in the 1Q25 release as a development that will impact all of FY25. Licensing income is volatile due to its lumpy nature, such as large initial payments or specific collaborative drops, which in FY23/24 included big names such as Ikea or Adidas.

The company says the impact will be 'significant' to licensing income in FY25. In FY24, licensing income reached EUR 3.5 million, which is almost entirely operating profit given the nearly 100% gross margin nature of the category. If the impact is 70%, like in 1Q25 (probably lower because 1Q24 had already booked 40% of licensing profit from the year), then the impact to operating profits will be EUR 2 million (or 6% of operating profit and 1pp of margin).

Costs/investment keep going up: Despite weak licensing and an uncertain macro, the company is maintaining or even increasing its investments, which can also be called costs.

Marketing spend, which reached EUR 10 million in FY24, is expected to grow further in FY25. In 1Q, Marimekko participated in the Copenhagen Fashion Week, opened three pop-up stores in Asia, three new permanent stores in Europe, and another in Tokyo (partner-operated), and also launched an itinerant exhibition in Asia. Marketing is in part used for all of these activities, but also to sustain sales via 'low-calorie' digital sales (i.e., digital sales that bump revenues but generate little profits because the CAC is high).

Headcount and SG&A also continued to increase. With sales largely flat and licensing down, this led to a 200bps decline in operating margins. The company frames this as a long-term brand-building strategy. While I understand the rationale, I remain skeptical of such moves in the context of a consumer discretionary environment that remains highly uncertain.

Valuation above the best of the industry, not attractive

Management maintained FY25 guidance, which expects sales to be flat to modestly up versus EUR 182 million in FY24. Surprisingly, operating margins are expected to be flat to slightly up (16–19% range, compared to 17.5% in FY24). Given the 1pp headwind from licensing alone and the expanding SG&A base, this seems optimistic. Unfortunately, the earnings call did not include questions addressing how this would be achieved, so I remain unconvinced.

Even taking the company’s guidance at face value, we are looking at EUR 30–35 million in operating income and EUR 22–25 million in net income. The company paid out most of its net income as dividends in FY24 (EUR 15 million), so most of the income becomes actual returns for shareholders.

Against this, Marimekko trades at a market cap of EUR 550 million, or 22 to 25 times earnings. This is very expensive for an apparel name operating at or near cyclical peak margins. Compare it to LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), probably the best higher-end brand operator in the world, trading at around 20x.

A high multiple is bad for two reasons. First, it leaves very limited room for upside from here unless global conditions improve significantly, and second, it opens a compound risk of worse economic conditions, worsening sales and margins (i.e. lower profits) coupled with multiple compression.

As I mentioned in my first article on the brand, I believe Marimekko is a quality brand with unique assets, a strong aesthetic identity, and a history of competent management. But it is not LVMH, and yet it trades at a higher multiple. I maintain my Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lands' End: At This Valuation, Hold Your Nose And Buy
data.description:
We have suggested several trades in Lands' End (NASDAQ:LE) over the last few years. Nearly all of them have worked out except for our last call for a swing trade which came just before the market sold off, and tariffs rocked retailers hard. Shares are down almost in half and while stop losses would have limited the damage, it was still a sight to behold. However, at this valuation, and all impacts of tariffs seemingly baked in, the stock is at a hold your nose and buy level. Many specialty retailers are in a similar boat. They face huge competition and the tariffs are a costly impact. But the valuation has eroded so much, especially in the last few months, shares are at a level where the risk-reward is incredibly favorable. These are the setups we model and look for, with extended research, entry points, and exits, at BAD BEAT Investing. LE stock is a decent candidate for a swing back to double-digit share prices, and would be double-digit trading returns from this level. Let us discuss the just-reported earnings, which have us bullish.

A specialty retailer, with a number of brands

This is a specialty retailer folks, with a number of different brands. Like competitors it offers its customers casual clothing, swimwear, shoes, and home products. There is of course the flagship banner Lands' End, but also brands like Lands' End Lighthouse, Drifter, and several others. Specialty retail is tough and for months, Lands' End, Inc. stock, as well as many competitors, have been crushed. But is it all that bad?

Q1 results in context

Looking at Q1 revenue, sales were still down 8.5% from last year hitting $261.2 million missing consensus by $11.4 million. As a reminder, this is really a digitally focused retailer and global online revenue was $227.7 million, a decrease of $1 million from $228.7 million a year ago. U.S. online revenue was $170.7 million, an increase of 0.2% from $170.5 million a year ago. There was ongoing strength in their outerwear products, but a slow start to the swimwear season. The “Outfitters” brand saw sales of $42.9 million, rising 0.5% from $42.7 million last year. This quarter, school uniform sales were a touch light, but business uniforms performed well. What about margin power?

Margins and earnings power

The company has made strong efforts to boost its margins. This has continued. Here in Q1, the company saw gross margin expansion of approximately 210 basis points, hitting 50.8% rising from 48.7% a year ago. But due to lower sales, total gross profit was lower. However, the margin expansion is impressive. Adjusted EBITDA was $9.5 million, down from $11.6 million a year ago. This was a result of lower sales primarily, while selling expenses and operational expenses were generally well-controlled. In terms of inventory, we saw strong inventory turnover. The company saw a 9% reduction in year-over-year inventory, suggesting that the company is efficiently moving products. The efficiency efforts and sped to market initiatives put into place have been paying off. This continues a run of significant inventory turnover and margin improvement. This is usually a money losing quarter, but less money was lost than last year which is positive. Adjusted net loss was $5.4 million of $0.18 per share. This is better than the losses of $6.2 million or $0.20 per share last year.

We also want to point out that despite the company losing money on an EPS basis, and cash balances coming down, the company still repurchased $2.8 million in shares. The balance sheet is in relatively good shape. There is $243 million in term loan debt outstanding, a $13 million improvement from a year ago, and $40 million of borrowings on its ABL facility (the same as a year ago).

Forward view, and a strategic plan

We like buying here again for a trade for two reasons. One, operationally and despite tariff risk, the company is in better shape than a year ago. And second we do know that the company is in the middle of a strategic alternative process which could include a sale or a merger. But without it, the outlook is good. A word on tariffs here. The company is assuming a baseline tariff of approximately 10% in all countries except China, which accounted for less than 8% of the company's product cost in 2024 and where we assume a 30% tariff. We surmise it is moving to be even less dependent on China moving forward. As we look ahead, for fiscal 2025, sales look to be $1.33 to $1.45 billion. That means the sales declines are likely done if the range hits toward the mid- to higher-end. That is positive. Adjusted EPS is guided at a wide $0.48 to $0.86. Folks, if it hits the midpoint of $0.67, the stock is trading at 12X FWD. That is a solid valuation. It also means growth of 65% from last year. We think you buy here at $8.

data.title: Vigil Neuroscience: ALSP Data Doesn't Deliver, But Sanofi Purchase Remains Intact
data.description:
Recently, Vigil Neuroscience, Inc. (NASDAQ:VIGL) announced an update relating to its development of fully human monoclonal antibody TREM2 agonist iluzanebart for the treatment of patients with adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal spheroids and pigmented glia [ALSP]. It was noted that this drug passed with flying colors when it came to safety and tolerability profile across both doses tested [20 mg/kg and 40 mg/kg] of it, but that it failed to show any beneficial effect when it came to possible biomarker improvement or other efficacy endpoints in the targeting of these patients.

With this failed phase 2 open-label IGNITE trial not delivering for the company, it is expected to return rights of iluzanebart back to Amgen (AMGN). The stock remains a "Hold" rating because despite this mid-stage trial failing, and Sanofi (SNY) is still expected to acquire Vigil Neurosciences for approximately $470 million. The whole point of the acquisition of this small-cap biotech actually had nothing to do with the development of iluzanebart at all. Instead, the whole premise was for Sanofi to get its hands on Vigil's oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's disease [AD].

This makes sense because, as I will show below, there was the release of positive phase 1 single-ascending dose [SAD] and multiple-ascending dose [MAD] study data released using this specific TREM2 agonist to treat these AD patients. With the continued acquisition of Vigil Neuroscience By Sanofi still on track for Q3 of 2025, plus the potential for shareholders to receive a Contingent Value Right [CVR] of $2.00 per share upon first commercialization of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD, I believe for these reasons that this stock remains a "Hold" rating.

Phase 2 Open-Label IGNITE Study In ALSP Doesn't Deliver Any Value For Shareholders

As I noted directly above, Vigil Neuroscience announced an update relating to its phase 2 open-label IGNITE study, which used fully human monoclonal antibody TREM2 agonist for the treatment of patients with adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal spheroids and pigmented glia. Before going over the mid-stage trial itself that was released by this company, it is important to mention what this disorder is and why it is highly devastating for these patients.

Adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal spheroids and pigmented glia [ALSP] is characterized as a leukoencephalopathy in which white matter of the brain is altered. This white matter consists of nerve fibers [axons] which are protected by myelin and transmit nerve impulses throughout the body. The alteration off white matter is shown as a lesion on the brain in an MRI, which is how it is known. The bottom line is that damage to myelin and neurons of a patient are common hallmarks of ALSP disease. Symptoms of this disorder vary a lot and can range from depression/memory loss all the way to loss of functional thinking and motor skills/muscle loss.

The phase 2 Open-label IGNITE study failed to deliver in terms of iluzanebart being able to help treat these patients. The company didn't share much in terms of clinical efficacy data at all. All that was noted was that this fully human monoclonal antibody TREM2 agonist didn't have an effect on improving biomarkers for these patients. Nor, was it shown that any clinical efficacy endpoints saw any type of major improvement. A positive would be at least that this drug given at both 20 mg/kg and 40 mg/kg doses was safe/tolerable for patients to take.

It is a shame how this program ended up, and the reason why I state that is because there was a lot of potential in place when the company first reported positive interim data from this IGNITE study back on November 16th of 2023. At that time, it noted that iluzanebart was able to show positive changes in terms of neurofilament light chain [NfL] and MRI biomarkers in terms of ventricular volume and gray matter volume as well.

The disorder of ALSP occurs due to a mutation of the CSF1R gene, which results in microglial dysfunction. Both TREM2 and CSF1R transduce biological effects of allowing for cell survival and proliferation. This effect is replicated through signaling partner DAP12/SYK. The goal of iluzanebart was to transduce signaling through this partner signaling agent and compensate for CSF1R loss observed in ALSP patients. The end result? To restore microglial function and reduce disease burden for these patients.

Unfortunately, this was not achieved as intended. With that being said, the company noted in its release of data, that it intends to discontinue the phase 2 long-term open-label study. As I noted before, the final outcome is that the company will return rights of iluzanebart back to Amgen before the closing of the acquisition of Vigil in Q3 of 2025.

Sanofi Acquisition Of Vigil Neuroscience Presses On Without Issue

The truth is that while it is disappointing that Vigil Neuroscience will not be able to continue developing iluzanebart for the treatment of patients with ALSP, it is not going to matter much if at all. Despite this phase 2 open-label IGNITE study failing, Sanofi is still in place to acquire Vigil. Again, it is solely doing so because of the ongoing development of oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927.

With that being said, the deal that was laid out was for this big pharmaceutical company to acquire Vigil Neuroscience for approximately $470 million. In other words, the deal itself entails shareholders of the small-cap company to receive up to $10.00 per share in cash. This is to split up as $8.00 per share in cash at closing of this transaction and then the $2.00 per share CVR to potentially be obtained upon the first commercial sale of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's disease [AD]. The global Alzheimer's therapeutics market size is expected to reach $15.19 billion by 2030. If everything goes accordingly, then Sanofi should complete its acquisition of Vigil Neuroscience by Q3 of 2025.

It makes perfect sense why Sanofi would want to acquire Vigil Neuroscience for its oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927. Why would this be the case? There are actually two reasons why this is the case. The first reason being that this is a phase 2-ready clinical asset. In essence, it is ready to be evaluated in a mid-stage study targeting such a neurological disorder patient population.

Secondly, it is because, as I will be going over now, Vigil reported positive results from its phase 1 SAD and MAD study using VG-3927 to treat AD patients. The pharmacokinetic [PK] profile was ideal and lends the ability to have convenient once-daily oral dosing for these patients. More importantly, it was noted that soluble TREM2 [sTREM2] was reduced in a dose-dependent 50% manner in Cerebral Spinal Fluid [CSF]. Why is this an important finding? That's because sTREM2 activity on microglia [found on surface of microglial cells] is increased and leads to neuroinflammation. Thus, the goal is to reduce sTREM2 as much as possible.

The end goal for this particular drug is TREM2 agonism [increase in microglial function] to clear inflammation and pathologic aggregates in the patient's brain. The hope is that this will prove to be the case upon Sanofi taking over phase 2 testing of this oral small molecule TREM2 agonist drug.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Vigil Neuroscience had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $87.1 million as of March 31st of 2025. The truth is that it believed that this cash on hand would only be enough for a cash runway to fund its operations into 2026. The company was burning through approximately $23.5 million in cash per quarter. This was broken down into $16.5 million in R&D expenses and $7.0 million in G&A expenses. Had this biotech needed to continue its operations on its own, then it would definitely need to raise additional cash.

However, it is in a unique situation in that it is expected to be acquired by Sanofi in Q3 of 2025. With that being said, if the acquisition of Vigil by Sanofi closes at that expected time, then there would be no need to worry about the small-caps financial backing. Sanofi would be able to handle the expense of running a phase 2 study of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD on its own.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of with Vigil Neuroscience. The first risk is the acquisition of it by Sanofi. This transaction has not yet been completed, as it is not expected to close until Q3 of 2025. The risk here is that there is no certainty that this acquisition will occur. If Vigil ends up not being acquired, then it would be forced to advance VG-3927 on its own. Plus, it would be on the hook financially to do so.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expected phase 2 study of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's disease. Such a mid-stage trial was expected to start in Q3 of 2025 and upon eventual completion of it, there can be no assurance that this mid-stage trial is going to end up achieving statistical significance in its intended primary endpoint. Nor, that the data is going to be adequate enough for it to be able to get a green light from the FDA to move towards phase 3 clinical testing of this oral small molecule TREM2 agonist.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the competition that Sanofi might end up facing when it comes to the development of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD. There is Novartis (NVS), which is expected to initiate a phase 2 study using its fully human monoclonal antibody that selectively activates TREM2 to clear pathogens and other debris in AD patients. Such a mid-stage trial is expected to start in the 2nd half of 2025 and this could end up being a competing drug. Especially, since the mechanism of action [MOA] of this drug is to also establish microglial activation to reduce disease burden.

Conclusion

Vigil Neuroscience remains a Hold rating, and it is highly likely that it will be acquired by Sanofi in Q3 of 2025. Again, the main reason for the big pharma is doing so is in order to get its hands on the oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD. Despite the failure of the phase 2 open-label IGNITE study, in using iluzanebart for the treatment of patients with ALSP, the big pharma is still expected to acquire this small-cap pharmaceutical company. Sanofi intends to initiate a phase 2 study using VG-3927 to target patients with Alzheimer's Disease. I noted the risk about Novartis above with its TREM2 agonist VHB937 drug candidate, in terms of also being developed to target this very same neurodegenerative patient population. Well, the thing is that Vigil believes its oral small molecule TREM2 agonist might have advantages.

First and foremost, it is not a fully human antibody like other TREM2 agonists that have failed in the past. It is also stated to have developed this drug to not bind to soluble sTREM2. It is believed that this could reduce the risk of Amyloid-related Imaging abnormalities [ARIA] seen with anti-amyloid drugs being given to patients. The lack of sTREM2 binding is expected to counter this. No Fc domain region, compared to monoclonal antibodies [mAbs], could mean lack of ARIA. Lastly, small molecule inhibitors have a shorter half-life, which even if ARIA is observed, dosing could be adjusted to actually account for this. With the continuation of the acquisition of this company by Sanofi, plus the expected start of a phase 2 study using VG-3927 for the treatment of patients AD [both in Q3 of 2025], I believe that this stock remains a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dollar Tree Stores: Valuation Is Still Cheap Given The Growth Outlook
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Dollar Tree Stores (NASDAQ:DLTR) was a buy rating because the business was not only benefiting from the economic downturn but also had a cleaner business model after the liquidation of Family Dollar. The stock is up ~26% since my last write-up, but I think there’s more room to run. With the MPP strategy clearly working, macro tailwinds still intact, and margin expanding, I remain bullish. Valuation remains undemanding, and I believe the setup continues to favor multiple expansion as fundamentals strengthen.

1Q25 results update

1Q25 Dollar Tree net sales grew 11.3% y/y to $4.637 billion, driven by same-store sales growth [SSSG] of 1.7%, and notably it was entirely driven by a 2.1% increase in traffic, which suggests that the brand continues to be relevant to cost-conscious consumers. The average ticket fell slightly by 0.4%, reflecting fewer items per basket, but I wouldn’t be too worried about this since there was a stronger footfall. Additionally, DTLR's gross margin expanded 40 bps y/y to 38%, which, coupled with better cost control—enterprise-level SG&A improved 94 bps y/y as a percentage of sales—drove adj. EPS to $1.43.

Macro Backdrop

I spoke about how the macro backdrop was positive for DLTR previously, and fast forward to today, it's clear to me that the economic environment has remained in favor of DLTR. While there has been moderating inflation, the reality, as I see it, is that the impact of previous inflationary price increases has continued to exert pressure on household budgets. The tariff situation has only made things worse, as US consumers are the ones paying the tariffs. In some situations, the tariffs have made some importers stop/delay product imports, as it is no longer economical to do so. This results in undersupply of certain goods (local supply cannot meet demand), which results in price increases. The recent update by McKinsey on the US consumer base is a good read, and I strongly recommend readers to check it out.

The key chart here, I think, is the one showing that lower-income consumers are the most likely to trade down in response to tariffs. This elevated price sensitivity is an added layer of tailwind that works in favor of DLTR. And this is a reason why I think the multi-price point [MPP] strategy will continue to work very well.

In my prior update, I was positive on the multi-price point [MPP] strategy after seeing how the MPP stores outperform non-MPP stores. This 1Q25 result update, along with the macro development, instilled more confidence in me that this MPP strategy is a structural growth driver. In the quarter, MPP stores again outpaced traditional ones, with SSSG about 200 bps higher, and what is even more impressive is that new stores with MPP enabled from day one saw a 15–20% total sales uplift. This tells me that there is still latent demand from value-conscious shoppers, and it makes a lot of sense for management to double down on this front. In 1Q25 alone, DLTR converted 966 additional stores to MPP, bringing the total to >5,000, which makes it well on its way to the 7,000 store target by the end of FY25. Based on the demand strength, if DLTR can continue to execute (convert/open stores as expected), there is a clear path ahead for DLTR to drive up SSSG via increased traffic and basket growth.

Gross Margin

Given the margin headwinds seen in 4Q24 (higher shrink and distribution costs), the Dollar Tree banner’s 1Q25 gross margin performance was, in my opinion, a clear positive. Gross margin improved to 38%, up from 37.6% in 1Q24, and this is despite continued shrink headwinds and a mix-shift toward lower-margin consumables. What I took away was that DLTR has a viable way to offset these headwinds so long as it continues to grow SSSG (drives up occupancy fixed cost leverage). And if DLTR can fix its shrink issue, DLTR's gross margin profile should be structurally improved. Combating against shrink is a tough thing to do, and as much as I like to see improvement here, I think DLTR delivering gross margin expansion in 1Q25 sends a positive message to the market that gross margin can continue to expand.

That said, one area to consider here is that risk from Section 301 tariff reinstatement, which management noted could hit 2H25 gross margin by up to 120 bps. This may not sound like a big hit to gross margin, but remember DLTR is a fixed-cost business, and its adj. net margin is only ~6%. As such, the 120 bps impact to gross margin, adjusted for tax, could easily translate into a 90 bps hit to net margin (or 15% hit to earnings).

Capital Returns

One area that would make me more bullish on DLTR is the return of capital to shareholders. In 1Q25, DLTR repurchased 1.15 million shares for $149.6 million and still has a sizeable $1.05 billion remaining under its current authorization. That equates to about 0.6% per quarter, or ~2.5% annualized if the trend holds for the rest of the year, which is alright, but it is not that amazing yet.

However, with DLTR now operating under a cleaner model, its MPP strategy is working very well in lifting SSSG, and with gross margin returning to expansion, DLTR should start to see a structurally better free cash flow [FCF] profile that gives it more flexibility to buy back shares.

Valuation

Although the share price has risen by a fair bit since my last update, I don’t think the valuation is showing expensive at all. DLTR is still trading at near its -1 standard deviation over the past 10 years (16x NTM PE). And this is despite a cleaner business model, a continuous favorable macro backdrop, and a solid strategy [MPP] that provides a clear path ahead for SSSG improvement. I believe this is too cheap. Dollar General Corp. (DG), with a similar expected growth outlook (mid-single-digits), trades at a way higher multiple: it trades at 19x. I actually think DG could be worth a lot more than 19x since it has the potential to accelerate revenue growth to >10% (1Q25 Dollar Tree Banner growth was 11.3%). For reference, Five Below (FIVE) trades at 25x with a low-teens revenue growth outlook.

Risk

Shrink is still a significant issue, and if it gets worse, gross margin could erode by a lot more. The growing mix of lower-margin consumables also adds pressure to margin, especially if discretionary categories don’t rebound. Lastly, tariff-related risks are real. Any reinstatement or escalation could cut into gross margins.

Conclusion

My view for DLTR is a buy rating. The 1Q25 results reinforced my confidence in the business—SSSG is healthy, gross margins are expanding despite shrink and mix headwinds, and the MPP rollout is clearly driving incremental traffic and sales lift. With macro tailwinds still favorable, a cleaner business model post-Family Dollar, and strong execution on strategic initiatives, DLTR is well-positioned for continued growth.
